message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectiv</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To explore the link between COVID-19 incidence, socio-economic covariates, and NHL incidence.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Ecological study design.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Sardinia, Italy.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We used official reports on the total cases of COVID-19 in 2020, published data on NHL incidence, and socio-economic indicators by administrative unit, covering the whole regional population.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main outcomes and measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We used multivariable regression analysis to explore the association between the natural logarithm (ln) of the 2020 cumulative incidence of COVID-19 and the ln-transformed NHL incidence in 1974-2003, weighing by population size and adjusting by socioeconomic deprivation and other covariates.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The cumulative incidence of COVID-19 increased in relation to past incidence of NHL (
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    < 0.001), socioeconomic deprivation (
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    = 0.006), and proportion of elderly residents (
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    < 0.001) and decreased with urban residency (
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    = 0.001). Several sensitivity analyses confirmed the finding of an association between COVID-19 and NHL.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This ecological study found an ecological association between NHL and COVID-19. If further investigation would confirm our findings, shared susceptibility factors should be investigated among the plausible underlying mechanisms.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Strengths and limitations of this study</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>This study exploited the availability of incidence data for non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma over a 30-year time frame and the 2020 cumulative incidence data for COVID-19 available for all administrative units in the Sardinia region, Italy.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Using an ecological study design, we observed that COVID-19 occurrence increased with past incidence of non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Limitations of the ecological study design require further investigation for confirmation and for identification of susceptibility factors possibly shared between the two diseases.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: COVID-19 and non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma: a common susceptibility pattern?

reference.article-title: [Lessons learned from the ‘Quirra syndrome’: epidemiology? No, thanks]
